The Tipping Point

A couple years ago, while attending a corporate conference in Northern Cali=
fornia, I spotted Roger Goodell standing across a vacant ballroom between e=
xecutive sessions. He was leaning up against a high top, minding his iPhone=
, diligently wearing his name tag, and keeping to himself as everyone else =
evacuated to the coffee stations=E2=80=94a guy very comfortable in his own =
skin, I reckoned.

It was a timely moment for an executive who had spent his tenure expanding =
the NFL across the week, the calendar, and the globe. The league had recent=
ly begun its $1 billion annual partnership with Amazon Prime, Thursday Nigh=
t Football, and I used the idle moment to introduce myself and express my e=
nthusiasm for the broadcast. For years, the league=E2=80=99s Thursday night=
 game had been an afterthought=E2=80=94usually a contest between lowly pret=
enders.
After all, the most established clubs could hardly have wanted to shorten t=
heir week of preparation to play a game on Thursday evenings, competing aga=
inst primetime programming on the other networks. If Monday Night Football =
was the panacea, Thursdays were the pits.

By that point, though, the league had ostensibly revived the product with e=
xcellent production, new talent, and an increasingly compelling schedule. G=
oodell, a mild-mannered guy with the firmest handshake I=E2=80=99ve ever be=
en a party to, politely nodded like a mensch. And what I remember most is t=
hat he had that inimitable executive talent of making a conversation partne=
r feel like he appreciated their perspective, regardless of whether it held=
 any merit.

Anyway, this memory bubbled up in my mind earlier this week as I was exchan=
ging notes with my partner John Ourand. Last week, John told me, the NFL ha=
d achieved a meaningful milestone for our replatforming age: The viewership=
 for
Amazon=E2=80=99s Thursday Night Football had beaten out ESPN=E2=80=99s Mond=
ay Night Football for the first time ever, 13.63 million to 12.2 million=E2=
=80=94the first time that a streaming service had attracted a larger audien=
ce than one of the league=E2=80=99s top partners in a same-week battle.

Yes, we=E2=80=99re all living through an enormous and unforgiving platform =
shift as our monoculture is replaced by a more affinity-driven, balkanized =
media mix that is still presenting itself with quirks aplenty. CNN=E2=80=99=
s ratings are falling, readers are fleeing The Washington Post, Rachel Madd=
ow=E2=80=99s post-election viewership has cratered, and some pundits are se=
riously, if shallowly, questioning whether Kamala Harris=E2=80=99s decision=
 to bypass Joe Rogan cost her the election. (Come on, people=E2=80=A6)

Nevertheless, these NFL numbers set off a minor supernova within the sports=
 media industrial complex. Many legacy media executives grumbled that the l=
eague was
favoring a new partner over longtime relationships=E2=80=94offering better =
games between more popular teams=E2=80=94and others felt this tipped the sc=
ales even further in favor of the insurgents. Given the ascent of the Prime=
 broadcast, some expressed skepticism that the Shield=E2=80=99s historic li=
near distributors would be able to renew their deals when the NFL inevitabl=
y exercises an out in its current contracts and reopens bidding at the end =
of the decade. As John notes in his excellent story, Is the NFL Falling Har=
d for Amazon? ( https://puck.news/will-the-nfl-put-more-games-on-amazon-pri=
me/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F16%2F24%29&utm_conte=
nt=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F16%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_acti=
on&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), the league=E2=
=80=99s partnership prerogatives=E2=80=94who they want to work with, which =
entities receive the choicest games, and who they exclude=E2=80=94will defi=
ne the next chapter of the content
industry.

And yet, it would be facile and plainly incorrect to assume that this next =

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">A couple years ago,=
 while attending a corporate conference in Northern California, I spotted <=
b>Roger Goodell </b>standing across a vacant ballroom between executive ses=
sions. He was leaning up against a high top, minding his iPhone, diligently=
 wearing his name tag, and keeping to himself as everyone else evacuated to=
 the coffee stations=E2=80=94a guy very comfortable in his own skin, I reck=
oned. <br/><br/>It was a timely moment for an executive who had spent his t=
enure expanding the NFL across the week, the calendar, and the globe. The l=
eague had recently begun its $1 billion annual partnership with Amazon Prim=
e, <i>Thursday Night Football</i>, and I used the idle moment to introduce =
myself and express my enthusiasm for the broadcast. For years, the league=
=E2=80=99s Thursday night game had been an afterthought=E2=80=94usually a c=
ontest between lowly pretenders.
After all, the most established clubs could hardly have wanted to shorten t=
heir week of preparation to play a game on Thursday evenings, competing aga=
inst primetime programming on the other networks. If <i>Monday Night Footba=
ll </i>was the panacea, Thursdays were the pits.<br/><br/>By that point, th=
ough, the league had ostensibly revived the product with excellent producti=
on, new talent, and an increasingly compelling schedule. Goodell, a mild-ma=
nnered guy with the firmest handshake I=E2=80=99ve ever been a party to, po=
litely nodded like a mensch. And what I remember most is that he had that i=
nimitable executive talent of making a conversation partner feel like he ap=
preciated their perspective, regardless of whether it held any merit.<br/><=
br/>Anyway, this memory bubbled up in my mind earlier this week as I was ex=
changing notes with my partner <b>John Ourand</b>. Last week, John told me,=
 the NFL had achieved a meaningful milestone for our replatforming age: The=
 viewership for
Amazon=E2=80=99s <i>Thursday Night Football </i>had beaten out ESPN=E2=80=
=99s <i>Monday Night Football </i>for the first time ever, 13.63 million to=
 12.2 million=E2=80=94the first time that a streaming service had attracted=
 a larger audience than one of the league=E2=80=99s top partners in a same-=
week battle. <br/><br/>Yes, we=E2=80=99re all living through an enormous an=
d unforgiving platform shift as our monoculture is replaced by a more affin=
ity-driven, balkanized media mix that is still presenting itself with quirk=
s aplenty. CNN=E2=80=99s ratings are falling, readers are fleeing <i>The Wa=
shington Post</i>, <b>Rachel Maddow</b>=E2=80=99s post-election viewership =
has cratered, and some pundits are seriously, if shallowly, questioning whe=
ther <b>Kamala Harris</b>=E2=80=99s decision to bypass <b>Joe Rogan </b>cos=
t her the election. (Come on, people=E2=80=A6)<br/><br/>Nevertheless, these=
 NFL numbers set off a minor supernova within the sports media industrial c=
omplex. Many legacy media executives grumbled that the league was
favoring a new partner over longtime relationships=E2=80=94offering better =
games between more popular teams=E2=80=94and others felt this tipped the sc=
ales even further in favor of the insurgents. Given the ascent of the Prime=
 broadcast, some expressed skepticism that the Shield=E2=80=99s historic li=
near distributors would be able to renew their deals when the NFL inevitabl=
y exercises an out in its current contracts and reopens bidding at the end =
of the decade. As John notes in his excellent story, <i><u><a href=3D"https=
://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dUTlRoXzl2=
VGtXYmVocW5MVnYxOD0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3Mvd2lsbC10aGUtbmZsLXB=
1dC1tb3JlLWdhbWVzLW9uLWFtYXpvbi1wcmltZS8_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPVRoZStCYWNrc3Rvcn=
krLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMSUyRjE2JTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1UaGUrQ=
mFja3N0b3J5Ky0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4MTElMkYxNiUyRjI0JTI5XHUwMDI2dXRtX21lZGl1=
bT1lbWFpbF9hY3Rpb25cdTAwMjZ1dG1fc291cmNlPWN1c3RvbWVyLmlvXHUwMDI2dXRtX3Rlcm0=

5rX2lkIjoxOTc3ODQwfQ/e85e74d7c7b1ab60b72d03bb39e441e647a6ad77c1a601362d65e7=
51abec7cf4" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" c=
lass=3D"sdf">Is the NFL Falling Hard for Amazon?</a></u></i>, the league=E2=
=80=99s partnership prerogatives=E2=80=94who they want to work with, which =
entities receive the choicest games, and who they exclude=E2=80=94will defi=
ne the next chapter of the content
industry.<br/><br/>And yet, it would be facile and plainly incorrect to ass=
ume that this next chapter is already written. As my partner <b>Matt Bellon=
i </b>notes in his characteristically brilliant piece, <i><u><a href=3D"htt=
ps://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dUTlRoXz=
l2VGtXYmVocW5MVnYxOD0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvYm9iLWlnZXJzLW5ld=
GZsaXgtbW9tZW50Lz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249VGhlK0JhY2tzdG9yeSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUy=
ODExJTJGMTYlMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PVRoZStCYWNrc3RvcnkrLStTVUJTQ1J=
JQkVSUyslMjgxMSUyRjE2JTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsX2FjdGlvblx1MD=
AyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ1dG1fdGVybT1mNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxM=
mEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImxpbmtfaWQiOjE5Nzc5ODN9/058=
76da9fa24e9b25f471f3fab67b058f6296ded81535a60cfee120a50128670" style=3D"col=
or:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">Bob Iger=E2=
=80=99s Netflix Moment</a></u></i>, <b>Iger </b>has completely reversed the=
 company=E2=80=99s fortunes in the streaming business over virtually the sa=
me brief period that Amazon has been broadcasting NFL games. When Iger took=
 back the reins from <b>Bob Chapek</b>, Disney was absorbing a staggering $=
1.5 billion <i>quarterly </i>loss in streaming. On last week=E2=80=99s earn=
ings call, the company announced third-quarter profits of more than $250 mi=
llion as it prepares to close on the final third of Hulu and roll out ESPN=
=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CFlagship=E2=80=9D D.T.C. service. <br/><br/>The
impact of the results, in profound ways, transcend the P&L. =E2=80=9CI =
think we can call another winner in the streaming wars,=E2=80=9D Matt wrote=
=2E =E2=80=9CNetflix, obviously, is alread=
y one of the three or four video se=
rvices that will survive this era of digital transition. YouTube is there, =
too. Spotify, if we count audio. And now, Disney almost certainly will make=
 it.=E2=80=9D Indeed, this battle to survive until the next stage of the in=
dustry is far more consequential than any sporting contest. It=E2=80=99s th=
e story of our time, and the macro-saga of our industry. And it=E2=80=99s c=
ertainly what you should expect to read in Puck.<br/><br/>Have a great week=
end,<br/>Jon<br/></td>
